text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old female with a known history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (KRAS G12V mutant, SMAD4 retained, TMB-low, MSS). She was initially treated with 8 cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX followed by concurrent long-course chemoradiation (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions via IMRT with concurrent capecitabine), completed approximately 6 weeks prior to this examination. This study is obtained for restaging prior to surgical planning and multidisciplinary tumor board review.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner using a dedicated pancreatic protocol. Non-contrast, arterial phase (20 seconds post-injection), pancreatic parenchymal phase (40 seconds), and portal venous phase (70 seconds) acquisitions were obtained following intravenous administration of 120 mL of nonionic iodinated contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL) at a rate of 4 mL/second. Thin-section axial reconstructions (0.625 mm) were obtained through the pancreatic bed. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats were reviewed. Oral contrast was administered. Images are compared with the prior pancreatic protocol CT obtained after 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX, as well as the baseline pre-treatment study.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS AND PRIMARY TUMOR:
In the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, there persists a zone of hypoattenuating soft tissue with poorly marginated borders measuring approximately 2.3 cm in greatest axial dimension, decreased from 2.6 cm on the prior restaging CT (obtained after completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX) and substantially reduced from the baseline measurement of 3.4 cm at initial diagnosis. The conspicuity of the discrete tumor mass is somewhat reduced compared to prior studies, with the previously defined hypodense nodular configuration now appearing more heterogeneous and ill-defined in morphology. This evolution in character likely reflects a combination of residual viable tumor and post-treatment fibrotic and inflammatory changes, and it is not possible on CT imaging alone to reliably distinguish between the two. There is surrounding soft tissue thickening and mild peripancreatic stranding, which is substantially attributed to post-radiation treatment effect. The main pancreatic duct proximal to the lesion continues to demonstrate mild upstream dilatation, measuring approximately 4 mm, slightly improved compared to 5 mm on the baseline study. The uninvolved pancreatic body and tail demonstrate preserved parenchymal attenuation without atrophic change.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
The relationship between the soft tissue mass and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) has changed favorably since baseline. On multiplanar reformatted images, the degree of circumferential soft tissue contact with the SMA is now estimated at approximately 80 to 90 degrees, representing a meaningful improvement compared to the estimated 120 degrees of abutment noted on the post-mFOLFIRINOX restaging CT and substantially reduced from the original abutment exceeding 180 degrees at diagnosis. There is no longer circumferential encasement of the SMA. The fat plane between the soft tissue density and the SMA lateral wall appears at least partially reconstituted along the posterior and right lateral aspects, though some residual soft tissue proximity remains anterolaterally. No gross arterial irregularity or stenosis of the SMA is identified.

With respect to the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), the previously noted abutment with partial luminal narrowing has continued to improve. The SMV now demonstrates only mild abutment along its left lateral wall without convincing luminal compromise or distortion. The vessel retains its rounded caliber without teardrop deformity. The SMV-portal vein confluence appears patent. The portal vein is of normal caliber without evidence of thrombosis or compression. The hepatic artery proper and its right and left branches demonstrate normal enhancement and caliber without encasement or stenosis. The celiac axis appears unremarkable.

No collateral venous formation or cavernous transformation is identified to suggest occult venous obstruction.

LIVER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Hepatic parenchymal attenuation is homogeneous throughout all phases of enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on this study. No hypodense, arterially enhancing, or washout-demonstrating lesions are seen in any hepatic segment. The common bile duct measures 5 mm in caliber, within normal limits, unchanged from prior study. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without cholelithiasis or wall thickening.

SPLEEN:
Normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation throughout all phases. No focal splenic lesions.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and configuration. No adrenal masses or nodules.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size with symmetric cortical thickness and enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No calculi.

BOWEL:
Small bowel and colon appear unremarkable without obstruction, perforation, or wall thickening. No pneumatosis. The small bowel loops in the right upper and mid-abdomen demonstrate mild crowding in the expected mesenteric root region attributable to post-radiation perivascular fibrosis; no discrete mesenteric mass is identified. No bowel obstruction.

LYMPH NODES:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, periportal, celiac axis, superior mesenteric, para-aortic, or pelvic regions. Several small subcentimeter lymph nodes are present in the aortocaval and mesenteric regions, unchanged in size and morphology from prior study, most likely reactive or residual post-treatment nodes. No confluent adenopathy.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No evidence of peritoneal thickening, nodularity, or implants. No ascites. The mesentery demonstrates mild stranding in the root region, consistent with expected post-radiation inflammatory change.

VASCULATURE:
Aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber. No aneurysmal dilatation. No evidence of venous thromboembolism within the visualized vessels.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No destructive osseous lesions are identified in the visualized spine, ribs, or pelvis. Mild multilevel degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine, of chronic appearance. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions. Abdominal wall is intact.

CORRELATIVE LABORATORY DATA:
CA 19-9: 52 U/mL (compared to 85 U/mL at completion of mFOLFIRINOX and 1,200 U/mL at baseline; continued downward trend; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL).

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued favorable evolution of the pancreatic head/uncinate soft tissue density since the initiation of neoadjuvant therapy. The primary lesion now measures approximately 2.3 cm, reduced from 2.6 cm on the prior post-mFOLFIRINOX scan and from the baseline of 3.4 cm, representing a cumulative reduction of approximately 32% in greatest dimension over the full neoadjuvant treatment course. The lesion morphology has become less discretely mass-like and more confluent with surrounding soft tissue, reflecting a mixture of residual neoplasm and post-treatment fibrotic response that is difficult to disentangle on CT imaging.

2. The relationship between the soft tissue abnormality and the superior mesenteric artery has improved markedly over the course of treatment. Circumferential contact is now estimated in the range of 80 to 90 degrees, compared to greater than 180 degrees at baseline and 120 degrees following mFOLFIRINOX. This reduction in SMA abutment to less than 90 degrees is a meaningful change from the initial borderline resectable classification.

3. The superior mesenteric vein demonstrates only mild abutment without significant luminal narrowing or teardrop deformity, representing substantial improvement from the original 40% luminal narrowing noted at diagnosis.

4. No hepatic metastases, peritoneal implants, or pathologically enlarged regional lymph nodes are identified on this study.

5. The ongoing decline in CA 19-9 (now 52 U/mL, down from 1,200 U/mL at baseline) is concordant with the imaging findings and supports continued treatment response.

Overall, findings indicate a continued and meaningful response to the multimodality neoadjuvant treatment program. The vascular anatomy, particularly with respect to the SMA, now appears potentially favorable for surgical exploration. Clinical and multidisciplinary surgical correlation is warranted for operative planning.

Signing Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging / Body CT]
Dictated and electronically signed.
